items.question: Who introduced the entropy measure used in information theory?
items.answer: Claude Shannon, in his 1948 paper "A Mathematical Theory of Communication," defined H(X) = −Σ p log p as the average uncertainty of a discrete source—borrowing the name from statistical mechanics but giving it a precise information-theoretic meaning.

items.question: What is Shannon entropy H(X)?
items.answer: H(X) = −Σ_x p(x) log p(x), the expected self-information of a discrete random variable X. It measures average surprise in bits (log base 2) or nats (natural log) per observation.

items.question: When is entropy equal to zero?
items.answer: When X is deterministic: one outcome has probability 1 and all others have probability 0. Then every observed outcome has zero surprise and H(X) = 0.

items.question: Where does entropy appear in machine learning workflows?
items.answer: In decision-tree split criteria (information gain), cross-entropy classification loss, language-model perplexity, feature-selection mutual information, and analysis of label/class balance in datasets.

items.question: Why use −log p(x) instead of simply p(x) or 1 − p(x)?
items.answer: −log p(x) assigns more surprise to rare events and is the unique choice (up to scaling) that makes self-information additive for independent events and matches optimal code lengths in Shannon's source coding theorem.

items.question: How do you compute H(X) from a PMF in code?
items.answer: Sum −p(x) log p(x) over all x with p(x) > 0. In Python: filter positive probabilities, apply np.log2 or np.log, multiply by p, negate the sum. sklearn.metrics.entropy accepts count vectors directly.

items.question: Cause: distribution becomes more uniform over the same set of outcomes. Effect on H(X)?
items.answer: Entropy increases toward the maximum log n. Equal probabilities maximize uncertainty; moving from a skewed PMF toward uniform raises H(X).

items.question: Cause: one outcome's probability approaches 1. Effect on H(X)?
items.answer: Entropy decreases toward 0. The process becomes nearly deterministic, so average surprise vanishes.

items.question: Cause: a decision tree split produces pure child nodes. Effect on information gain?
items.answer: Information gain equals the parent entropy. Child entropies are 0, so parent H − weighted child H = parent H— the split fully explains the label.
